figures.caption: Fig. 1: Overview of multi-property monitoring framework. (a) A unified computation graph built from multiple properties. (b) A double buffering implementation for storing and updating internal node states efficiently at runtime.
TABLE I: Comparison of multi-property monitoring
TABLE III: Impact of cross-property sharing (dense): execution time (s) for four scenarios with 10 properties over 1 000 000 timesteps.
Fig. 2: Object-oriented execution model where each computation node maintains its own local storage in memory.
Fig. 3: Multi-property monitor compiled from properties processing input vectors p1, . . . , pn and yielding output vectors y1, . . . , ym at each time step.
TABLE VII: Total wall-clock time (s) to monitor 30 PastMTL properties over 1 000 000 discrete-time steps. Speedup is relative to the Reelay-Sequential baseline for the same feeder type.
TABLE IV: Execution time (s) for single-property discrete-time monitoring. Reelay vs. LoomRV using JSON and binary feeders.
TABLE VI: Execution time (s): Reelay binary vs. LoomRV binary, single-property, dense-time.
TABLE V: Execution time (s): Reelay JSON vs. LoomRV JSON, single-property, dense-time.
Fig. 4: Architecture overview of the monitoring framework, illustrating the parsing and compilation pipeline, configuration interface, and runtime execution components.
Fig. 5: Node creation and lookup workflow across parser, context, and content-addressable database components.
TABLE VIII: Total wall-clock time (s) to monitor 30 PastMTL properties over 1 000 000 dense-time steps. Speedup is relative to the Reelay-Sequential baseline for the same feeder type.
Fig. 7: Zero-allocation double-buffered arena. (a) Global buffer roles and swap mechanism. (b) Initial position of pointers. (c) Progression of pointers.
